one_line_takeaway: The paper gives the first (and, under its stated assumptions, provably unique) admissible-and-unbiased sequential-testing framework for LLM watermark detection via e-processes, trading a small amount of raw detection power for anytime-valid Type I error control that existing p-value-based watermark detectors lack under streaming/online monitoring.
key_finding: Only the e-process-based methods (weight-adaptive, OG, average) maintain valid, non-inflating Type I error under sequential/repeated monitoring; the sum-based p-value baselines (h_ars, h_log, h*_gum with d=0.1 and d=0.01) show Type I error that 'quickly explodes' once tested sequentially, despite being asymptotically valid in the fixed-sample setting. Among the sum-based baselines, h_ars (Aaronson's original score) has the best raw power (lowest Type II error) but no sequential validity. Among the three proposed e-process variants, the OG and average e-processes perform similarly and beat the weight-adaptive one on simulated data, while on real OPT-1.3B text the weight-adaptive and average e-processes perform similarly and beat OG -- so the authors recommend the average e-process (Eq. 15) as the practical default, since it tracks the better of the two constituent methods across settings. The average e-process achieves power roughly comparable to, and in some settings (top panel of Figure 2, OPT-1.3B, temperature 0.5) even exceeding, the best sum-based baseline (h_ars) despite providing anytime/sequential validity that the baselines lack. Sum-based methods show inflated Type II error at low temperature / long sequences due to degenerate (near-deterministic) NTP distributions producing tokens independent of watermark randomness; e-process methods degrade monotonically instead. Under human-edit dilution (r up to 0.5), all methods' Type II error rises, but the proposed e-process methods remain competitive with the non-sequentially-valid baselines.
core_contribution: The paper reformulates LLM watermark detection as a sequential (online) hypothesis test of independence between generated tokens and the watermark's pseudo-random sequence, and builds this test using e-processes rather than classical p-value statistics. It (1) derives an information-theoretic bound (KL(QP||UP) = Ent(P), Proposition 1) showing the maximum achievable e-power for any watermarking scheme is bounded by the next-token-prediction entropy, independent of the specific scheme; (2) proves that, under a stated independence/super-uniformity assumption (Assumption 1) and given the natural filtration of the pivotal statistics, the e-process family it constructs (running products of predictable calibrators, Eq. 6-7) is the ONLY class of admissible and unbiased e-processes, and hence the only class of admissible sequential tests for this problem (Theorems 1 and 2, invoking Ramdas et al. 2020, Theorem 18); (3) proposes three concrete constructions of adaptive calibrators -- a weight-adaptive e-process using an adaptively-tuned mixing parameter lambda_t (Eq. 9-11), an 'online Grenander' (OG) e-process built from the online Grenander density estimator of Hore et al. (2026) (Eq. 12-14), and an arithmetic-average e-process combining the two (Eq. 15, the paper's recommended default); and (4) proves power-one/consistency results: Theorem 3 gives exponential growth of the nonadaptive e-process under an m-dependence assumption on the NTP sequence (Assumption 2) specific to the Gumbel-max watermark, and Theorem 4 gives exponential growth (consistency) of the weight-adaptive, OG, and average e-processes under an idealized i.i.d.-NTP setting (Assumptions 3-4).
threat_model: Not an adversarial security threat model in the classic sense -- the paper's setting is a statistical detection problem: an LLM provider embeds a watermark (the paper focuses on the Gumbel-max scheme of Aaronson 2023, deployed internally at OpenAI) and a detector must decide, from a token stream observed online/streaming (unbounded, unknown stopping time), whether the text is machine-generated (watermarked) or human-written, while controlling false-positive (Type I error) rate under arbitrary/adaptive stopping. The paper does explicitly motivates this by real misuse cases named in its intro (misinformation, academic plagiarism, automated fraudulent content) and by the practical need to detect AI-generated content from autonomous agents in real time. It also models a partial-adversary/robustness scenario: text that is a mix of watermarked LLM output and human edits, where a fraction r in {0.1, 0.3, 0.5} of tokens are replaced by non-watermarked (human or independently sampled) values after the first 50 tokens -- this is the paper's stand-in for an adversary trying to dilute/evade the watermark by partial post-hoc editing. There is no model of an adversary attacking the watermark key, the calibrator, or the detection algorithm itself; robustness is evaluated only against random human-edit dilution, not targeted evasion.
limitations: Explicitly stated by the authors in the Conclusion: (1) the theoretical characterization of optimal e-process constructions is incomplete, particularly with respect to attaining the theoretical upper bound on e-power (KL(QP||UP)=Ent(P)) derived in Section 3.3 -- the paper shows this bound is generally unreachable via pivotal-statistic-based e-values (Section 3.3: 'the e-power has to be reduced, even if P were known'); (2) design of statistically efficient pivotal statistics tailored to watermark mechanisms other than Gumbel-max is left to future work; (3) the strongest power-one guarantee (Theorem 4) relies on an admittedly 'quite unrealistic' assumption (Assumption 3: i.i.d. constant NTP vector P over all tokens); (4) Theorem 3's guarantee for the nonadaptive e-process is described by the authors themselves as possibly weak in practice because it must guard against adversarial NTP vectors within the delta-bounded simplex; (5) the human-editing robustness experiment tests only random dilution by i.i.d. edits, not a targeted/adaptive adversary trying to specifically evade detection; (6) empirical evaluation uses only OPT-1.3B (explicitly flagged by the authors as a small model relative to commercial LLMs) and only the Gumbel-max watermark scheme, so generality to other watermarks/larger models is asserted but not empirically demonstrated.

datasets_used: Simulated spike-NTP-distribution data: vocabulary size K=1,000, Delta_t ~ U(0.001, delta) for delta in {0.2, 0.5}, 700-token sequences, 1,000 repeated experiments
OPT-1.3B open-source LLM, generation setup following Kirchenbauer et al. (2023) and Li et al. (2025b), temperatures 0.5 and 1, 500 repeated experiments
Human-edit-dilution variant of the OPT-1.3B experiment: edit proportions r in {0.1, 0.3, 0.5} applied after token 50, temperature 0.5, 500 repeated experiments
quantitative_results: Sequential (repeated-monitoring) Type I error of sum-based baselines 'quickly explodes' (reaches up to roughly 0.3-0.5 in Figure 1/Figure 2 panels) while e-process methods keep sequential Type I error controlled at the nominal level across text lengths up to 600-700 tokens

Fixed-sample Type I error for all methods stays roughly in the 0.00-0.10 range in the simulated and OPT-1.3B experiments (Figures 1-2), but only e-process methods retain this control once tested sequentially

OG and average e-processes visibly outperform the weight-adaptive e-process on simulated data (Figure 1); weight-adaptive and average e-processes visibly outperform OG on real OPT-1.3B text (Figure 2)

Under human editing with r up to 0.5, Type II error rises for all methods but the proposed e-process methods remain 'comparable' in power to the non-sequentially-valid baselines (Figure 3, editing proportions 0.1/0.3/0.5)

At low temperature and long sequence length, sum-based methods show a 'slight inflation' in Type II error attributed to degenerate NTP distributions, whereas e-process methods show consistent monotonic Type II error reduction
baselines_compared: Sum-based statistic h_log(y) = log(y) (Fernandez et al., 2023; Kuditipudi et al., 2024)
Optimized sum-based statistic h*_gum,d with d=0.1 (Li et al., 2025b)
Optimized sum-based statistic h*_gum,d with d=0.01 (Li et al., 2025b)
(Proposed, compared against each other) weight-adaptive e-process with calibrator g(p)=-log(p)
